examples.title: Checking Basic Properties of a Binary Relation
examples.description: Demonstrates how to verify if a relation is reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric, and transitive using Python sets.
examples.explanation: We define four functions to check each property. For reflexivity, we verify every element maps to itself. For symmetry, every pair has its reverse. For antisymmetry, if both (a,b) and (b,a) exist with a≠b, it's not antisymmetric. For transitivity, we check all combinations where (a,b) and (b,c) exist, ensuring (a,c) also exists.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for transitive check, O(n) for others

examples.title: Checking Equivalence Relation
examples.description: Demonstrates verifying if a relation is an equivalence relation (reflexive, symmetric, and transitive) and finding equivalence classes.
examples.explanation: An equivalence relation must satisfy all three properties. We check each property sequentially. If verified, we find equivalence classes by grouping all elements related to each element. For congruence mod 3, elements {0,3,6...} form one class, {1,4,7...} another, etc.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for relation checking, O(n²) for finding classes

examples.title: Partial Order Relation and Hasse Diagram Basics
examples.description: Demonstrates checking if a relation is a partial order (poset), identifies cover relations, and determines maximal/minimal elements.
examples.explanation: A partial order (poset) requires reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive properties. The cover relation removes indirect connections to draw the Hasse diagram. Maximal elements have no greater element; minimal elements have no smaller element. For divisibility on {1,2,3,4,6,12}, 12 is maximal (only element nothing divides) and 1 is minimal (divides everything).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n³) for transitive and cover checks
